era_context: Late Bronze Age Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I is consolidating power and expanding into Syria. Egypt's 18th Dynasty is in the early New Kingdom period. Kassite Babylon is establishing itself as a regional power. Mycenaean Greece is in the Middle Bronze Age. This is a period of significant state formation, imperial competition, and military reorganisation across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's reign and Hittite expansion (broader context)
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I's reign (conventionally dated c. 1344–1322 BCE) represents the height of Hittite power in the Late Bronze Age. During this period, the Hittites expanded their control over much of Anatolia and northern Syria, establishing the Hittite Empire as one of the great powers of the Eastern Mediterranean. The specific events of 1621 BCE are not individually documented, but this year falls within the broader context of Hittite imperial consolidation and expansion.

sources.name: Hittite royal annals and treaties (later compilation, 13th century BCE)

events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign is conventionally dated c. 1344–1322 BCE, but absolute chronology for this period is debated. The assignment of events to 1621 BCE is speculative and depends on which chronological system is adopted.

events.title: Egyptian 18th Dynasty New Kingdom consolidation
events.description: Egypt's 18th Dynasty is consolidating New Kingdom power following the expulsion of the Hyksos. This period marks the beginning of Egyptian imperial expansion and the establishment of Egypt as a major Near Eastern power. The exact chronology is contested; conventional dating places the early 18th Dynasty c. 1550–1500 BCE, but alternative chronologies exist. This year falls within the broader era of Egyptian state reorganisation and military consolidation.
key_figures: Ahmose I or Amenhotep I (Egyptian pharaoh, depending on chronology)

sources.name: Egyptian king lists and temple inscriptions
Historiographic consensus on Egyptian New Kingdom chronology

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for the 18th Dynasty is debated. Conventional dating places the early 18th Dynasty c. 1550–1500 BCE, but some scholars propose dates as late as 1540–1510 BCE. The assignment to 1621 BCE is speculative.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional chronology for the 18th Dynasty and Hittite Empire is contested. Some scholars (proponents of the 'low chronology') place these events significantly later than traditional dating. The 'high chronology' places them earlier. No specific events are reliably attested for 1621 BCE itself; the entries above describe broader contextual processes. The reliability of assigning events to individual years in this period is extremely limited.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1621 BCE is extremely sparse. No contemporary written sources directly document events of this specific year. Knowledge comes from later compilations (royal annals, king lists), archaeological stratigraphy, and historiographic reconstruction. The chronology of the Late Bronze Age is notoriously uncertain, with debates spanning 100+ years. Egyptian and Hittite sources are biased toward royal and military narratives. The experiences of common people, trade networks, and non-state societies are largely invisible. Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, and the Americas have no written records for this period.

graph_edges.note: Hittite and Egyptian expansion occur in the same period, eventually leading to conflict.
